summary: I am Kelsey L. Pharr, Miami's Negro undertaker and welfare worker. Born in Salisbury, North Carolina nearly fifty years ago, I have lived in Miami for twenty-five years, dedicating myself to serving my people. I started as a waiter and bellhop at the Royal Palm Hotel in 1914, then bought the undertaking business with partners and eventually took it over myself. I also organized a funeral benefit society to protect our community from exploitative insurance schemes. Beyond business, I have been deeply involved in church and youth work, especially with the A.M.E. Zion Church, striving to uplift and educate our people.
cues: Miami resident for 25 years as of 1939

summary: I have lived in Miami since 1914, making it my home for over twenty-five years. It was here that I transitioned from working at the Royal Palm Hotel to becoming the city's Negro undertaker. Miami's colored town is where I established my funeral business and welfare work, and where I have been active in church and youth activities. The city also faces challenges like voodooism and fraudulent funeral insurance schemes, which I have worked to combat. Miami is the center of my community efforts and the place where I have raised my family.

I was born in Salisbury, North Carolina, nearly fifty years ago. My early years were spent there, attending the local Negro schools and working while going to college. My father worked in the railroad yards, and I was fortunate to attend a good Negro grade and high school, as well as the Negro Normal School and A.M.E. Zion College. Salisbury shaped my early education and values, and it was where I first became superintendent of the A.M.E. Sunday School at age sixteen. It was a place that gave me the foundation to later pursue higher education and serve my people.

My parents were born free in South Carolina, and three of my grandparents had been slaves there. Though I was born in North Carolina, my family's roots trace back to South Carolina. My wife also hailed from South Carolina and was a graduate of Clifton College. The heritage and history of South Carolina have been part of my family story and identity. It connects me to the struggles and progress of our people in the South.
cues: parents born free in South Carolina

summary: I have been deeply involved with the A.M.E. Zion Church since my youth. At sixteen, I became superintendent of the A.M.E. Sunday School in Salisbury and continued in that role until I left. In Miami, I serve as president of the society of my own A.M.E. Zion church, focusing on young people's spiritual life and social activities. For twenty-three years, I have led young people's work throughout the South in this denomination, helping to steer them away from superstition and harmful practices. The church has been central to my mission of uplifting and educating our community.
cues: president of church society in Miami
23 years leading youth work in the South

summary: I earned my Bachelor's degree at Trinity Methodist College before pursuing medical studies. Attending Trinity was a significant step in my education, though financial challenges made it difficult. I worked multiple jobs to support myself while studying there. This college prepared me for further medical training and my commitment to serve my race. It was an important milestone in my academic and professional journey.

After Trinity Methodist College, I entered Tufts University School of Medicine in Boston with the intention of serving my race as a medical professional. However, while in Miami working to finish my medical course, I shifted focus to the undertaking business. Though I did not complete my medical degree, my time at Tufts reflects my dedication to education and service. It was part of my broader effort to uplift my community through professional skills.

When I first came to Miami in 1914, I worked as a waiter and bellhop at the old Royal Palm Hotel. This job helped me support myself while I was finishing my medical studies. It was during this time that the local colored undertaker passed away, and I, along with three fellow waiters, bought the undertaking business. The Royal Palm Hotel was my entry point into Miami's working life and the stepping stone to my later career as an undertaker and community leader.

To improve my undertaking business, I took a six-week embalming course in New York State and passed the state examination. This certification allowed me to take full charge of the business upon returning to Miami. Passing the New York State embalming exam was crucial for my professional credibility and the expansion of my services. It demonstrated my commitment to providing quality funeral services to my community.

Mr. Roddy Burdine was a kind and generous friend who loaned me nine hundred dollars without any security except my word. This loan made it possible for me to expand my undertaking business and improve the services I offered. He was a man who quietly helped many, both white and black, without seeking recognition. I paid him back as quickly as I could, grateful for his trust and support that contributed to my success.

The Bahamas is one of the places from which voodooism has come to influence the Negro community here. I have observed that many of our people hold on to these superstitions, which come from places like the Bahamas, Jamaica, and especially Haiti. This cultural influence presents a challenge that the church and schools must address through education and religious instruction. We strive to lift our people out of superstition and towards the teachings of Christ.

Jamaica, along with the Bahamas and Haiti, is a source of voodooism that has taken hold among some Negroes in Miami. This superstition affects many and is something we actively resist through church and educational efforts. We work to replace these beliefs with Christian teachings and new ideals. Understanding these cultural roots helps us better serve and guide our community.

Haiti is especially notable as a source of voodooism that influences some in our Negro community. This superstition holds a strong grip because of natural tendencies toward superstition, and it is a major concern for us. We strive to counteract this through persistent religious instruction and education to turn hearts toward the true God. Haiti's cultural impact underscores the importance of our church and school programs.

Father Divine is a controversial figure with a large following here in Miami. I believe he was in Miami about a year ago, and many of our people, including some who are not colored, are carried away by him. In my opinion, he is just another racket, a scheme to exploit the credulous and easily impressed. We must counter such charlatans by educating our people through the church and school to cure them of their credulity.

Dade County is where many funeral insurance contracts require the death to occur for benefits to be paid, which is unfair to our people. I fought against these exploitative insurance schemes that operated without licenses and had restrictive conditions. In response, I organized a funeral benefit society that provided fair terms, including burial regardless of where death occurred. Dade County is the local jurisdiction where these struggles over funeral insurance and community welfare have taken place.

My wife was a graduate of Clifton College in South Carolina. She came from there before joining me in Miami. Her education at Clifton College was part of her background, and she passed away eleven years ago. Her connection to Clifton College is a part of our family's story and heritage.

My son is currently a senior at Northwestern University in Chicago and will graduate this coming June with music as his major. He has already had auditions in Hollywood and is signed up for a concert tour. This achievement makes me proud, especially since he only attended grade school here in Miami before going north for higher education. His success reflects the progress and opportunities we strive for in our family and community.

Hollywood is where my son has already had auditions as part of his music career. Though he was educated mostly outside Miami after grade school, Hollywood represents an important step in his professional development. It is a place connected to his aspirations in music and performance. His involvement there signals the broader reach of our family's ambitions beyond Miami.

entity: Booker T. Washington High School (Miami, Florida)
summary: When my son was young, the colored high school in Miami was not good enough, so he only attended grade school here. However, I have worked energetically to improve the Booker T. Washington High School, where now all fifty-four teachers have college degrees and are working toward life certificates. The school has seen a rise in standards under Principal Moseley Meredith, who succeeded Austin. This progress gives us hope that future generations can attend school here with confidence.

Moseley Meredith was appointed principal of Booker T. Washington High School, succeeding Austin. Under his leadership, the school has improved significantly, with all teachers holding college degrees and working toward life certificates. I have supported his efforts to raise educational standards in Miami's colored schools. His work is vital to providing quality education for our children.
cues: principal of Booker T. Washington High School

summary: Austin was the principal of Booker T. Washington High School before Moseley Meredith took over. During his tenure, the school had lower standards, which I found insufficient for our children. The change in leadership to Meredith has brought positive developments. Austin's period is part of the school's history as it worked toward improvement.

Rev. Daniel Iverson, pastor of the Shenandoah Presbyterian Church, is a respected leader who helped connect me with the Federal Writers' Project interview. He also chairs the Conference on Interracial Relations, working to arbitrate differences and support colored workers in Miami. The church and its leadership play an important role in fostering interracial understanding and community welfare. I appreciate their efforts and the trust placed in them by both white and colored communities.

Daniel Iverson is the pastor of Shenandoah Presbyterian Church and chairman of the Conference on Interracial Relations here in Miami. He is a southerner from an old South Carolinian family who has been associated with Negro communities all his life. His understanding and leadership have been vital in mediating racial differences and supporting colored workers. He was the one who furnished my name for this interview, reflecting his role as a bridge between communities.
cues: chairman of Conference on Interracial Relations

summary: The Conference on Interracial Relations is an organization of white and colored workers in Miami that seeks to arbitrate differences and help colored workers through advice and cooperation with officials. I am connected to this group through Rev. Daniel Iverson, its chairman. The conference plays an important role in promoting understanding and improving conditions for our people. Their work complements my own efforts in welfare and community uplift.
cues: organization of white and colored workers
